skill.name: playwright-best-practices
skill.description: Reviews Playwright TypeScript tests against stability, locator, assertion, fixture, sharding, and API mocking best practices.
skill.summary: Review Playwright TypeScript tests for reliable, maintainable automation patterns.
skill.author: vitalics

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command, network, filesystem, environment, and credential patterns, but review found they are primarily Markdown examples in Playwright guidance rather than executable skill code. The confirmed concern is that SKILL.md instructs the agent to fetch mutable remote guidelines before each review, which creates a supply-chain and prompt-injection exposure. No evidence of malicious exfiltration or prompt-injection text was found in the local skill files.

medium_findings.title: Mutable Remote Guideline Fetch
medium_findings.description: SKILL.md tells the agent to fetch fresh guidelines from a GitHub URL before each review and to treat the fetched content as the rule and output source. This is legitimate for freshness, but a changed or compromised remote file could alter the agent review instructions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly requires WebFetch of mutable remote instructions. I found no malicious payload locally, but the design creates a clear supply-chain and prompt-injection exposure.

low_findings.title: Static Findings Are Mostly Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The command execution, filesystem, environment, browser storage, and credential-related scanner hits occur inside documentation or Playwright code examples. They demonstrate test patterns and are not executed by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative high-risk static hits were verified in fenced examples and explanatory guidance. The files are Markdown guidance, not executable scripts loaded by the marketplace runtime.

low_findings.title: User File Reading Is Expected But Sensitive
low_findings.description: The skill asks the agent to read user-specified files or patterns for review. This is required for code review, but users should avoid submitting secrets or unrelated private files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The local instructions clearly require reading selected files. This is expected behavior for a review skill, with privacy risk limited by user-selected scope.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Prompt Content Used As Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on externally fetched guideline content for rules and output format. Pinning a commit or bundling the guideline file would reduce the risk of remote instruction drift.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present in SKILL.md. The risk is architectural rather than evidence of active malicious behavior.

content.user_title: Review Playwright Tests Against Best Practices
content.value_statement: Playwright suites often become flaky when tests use manual waits, fragile selectors, or shared state. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to review tests against practical TypeScript Playwright rules.
actual_capabilities: Reviews Playwright TypeScript tests for stability, speed, locator quality, assertions, fixtures, sharding, and API mocking.
Flags manual waits and recommends Playwright auto-waiting or specific event waits.
Recommends role-based locators, web-first assertions, and maintainable page object patterns.
Checks parallel execution patterns for shared state, worker isolation, and sharding issues.
Produces concise file and line oriented findings for user-supplied test files.
limitations: The user must provide files or patterns to review.
The skill instructions fetch mutable guidelines from GitHub before each review.
It does not execute Playwright tests or confirm browser behavior by itself.
The guidance is focused on Playwright with TypeScript and may not fit other test frameworks.

use_cases.title: Reduce flaky Playwright tests
use_cases.description: Review test files for manual waits, non-retrying assertions, shared state, and brittle timing assumptions.

use_cases.title: Improve test code reviews
use_cases.description: Apply a consistent checklist for locators, assertions, page objects, fixtures, and parallel execution before merging test changes.

use_cases.title: Refactor large Playwright suites
use_cases.description: Identify repeated patterns that should move into page objects, fixtures, or reusable mock helpers.

prompt_templates.title: Review one test file
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a focused file
prompt_templates.prompt: Review tests/example.spec.ts for Playwright best practices. Focus on flaky waits, locators, and assertions.

prompt_templates.title: Check a feature folder
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the Playwright tests under tests/checkout. Report issues by file and line, and suggest concise fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Audit parallel readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Review tests/**/*.spec.ts for parallel execution problems. Focus on shared users, shared files, test ordering, and sharding safety.

prompt_templates.title: Assess suite architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Playwright suite architecture. Evaluate page objects, fixtures, API mocking, locator strategy, and assertion quality.

output_examples.input: Review a checkout test file that uses fixed delays before clicking buttons.
output_examples.output: The review reports manual wait usage, explains why it can cause flakiness, and recommends auto-waiting actions or specific response waits.

output_examples.input: Review a login suite with repeated selectors and shared setup logic.
output_examples.output: The review highlights selector duplication, suggests a login page object, and recommends a fixture for reusable authenticated setup.

output_examples.input: Review a CI suite before enabling sharding.
output_examples.output: The review identifies shared test data, order dependencies, and storage state reuse that could fail under parallel execution.

best_practices: Prefer Playwright auto-waiting and web-first assertions before adding explicit waits.
Use role-based locators and accessible names before CSS selectors or XPath.
Keep reusable page behavior in page objects or fixtures when multiple tests share it.
anti_patterns: Do not add fixed sleeps to hide timing problems in tests.
Do not rely on DOM structure selectors when a user-facing role or label exists.
Do not share mutable users, files, or database state across parallel tests.

faq.question: What does this skill review?
faq.answer: It reviews Playwright TypeScript tests for stability, speed, locators, assertions, fixtures, sharding, and API mocking practices.

faq.question: Does it run my Playwright tests?
faq.answer: No. It reviews source files and gives guidance. You must run tests separately to verify behavior.

faq.question: Can it fix the tests automatically?
faq.answer: It can suggest fixes. Apply changes only after confirming they match your application and test environment.

faq.question: Why does it prefer role-based locators?
faq.answer: Role-based locators match how users and assistive technology identify controls, so they are usually more stable.

faq.question: Is it useful for CI performance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for parallel execution, sharding, fixtures, and reducing slow waits.

faq.question: What should I provide when using it?
faq.answer: Provide a Playwright test file, folder, or glob pattern, plus any specific concern such as flakiness or sharding.
